title: Biology Core
description: Cells, genetics, evolution, and ecosystems. How life works at every scale from molecule to biosphere.

lessons.title: Water & the Chemistry of Life
lessons.content:
Life as we know it depends entirely on water. A water molecule (H₂O) is polar — oxygen pulls electrons more strongly than hydrogen, creating a partial negative charge on oxygen and partial positive charges on the hydrogens.

This polarity allows water molecules to form `hydrogen bonds` with each other and with other polar molecules. Hydrogen bonds are individually weak but collectively powerful — they give water its remarkable properties.

`Cohesion` is the attraction between water molecules. It allows water to form droplets and move up plant stems against gravity (capillary action). `Adhesion` is water's attraction to other polar surfaces.

Water has high `specific heat` — it resists temperature change, stabilizing the internal environment of cells and moderating climates. It also has high `heat of vaporization`, meaning evaporation (like sweating) removes significant heat.

Water is the `universal solvent`. Ionic compounds and polar molecules dissolve in it because water molecules surround and separate the ions. Nonpolar molecules like fats do not dissolve — they are `hydrophobic`. Polar and ionic solutes are `hydrophilic`.

Water is also ionized slightly into H⁺ and OH⁻. The `pH scale` (0–14) measures hydrogen ion concentration. pH 7 is neutral, below 7 is acidic, above 7 is basic. `Buffers` resist pH change by absorbing or releasing H⁺, keeping biological systems in a narrow pH range.

examples.label: Key Properties of Water
examples.code:
Cohesion       → surface tension, capillary action
Adhesion       → water clings to glass, plant cell walls
High sp. heat  → resists temperature change
High heat of vap → cooling by evaporation
Universal solvent → dissolves polar/ionic substances
Expansion on freezing → ice is less dense than liquid water

questions.q: A solution has a pH of 4. Which statement is correct?
options: It has more OH⁻ ions than H⁺ ions
It is ten times more acidic than a pH 5 solution
It has the same H⁺ concentration as pH 7
questions.explanation: The pH scale is logarithmic. Each unit decrease means 10× more H⁺. pH 4 has 10× more H⁺ than pH 5, and it is acidic (below 7).

questions.q: Which property of water allows trees to pull water from roots to leaves dozens of meters above ground?
questions.explanation: Water molecules stick to each other (cohesion) and to the polar walls of xylem vessels (adhesion), creating a continuous column that tension can pull upward.

questions.q: Why does ice float on liquid water?
options: Ice molecules move faster than liquid water molecules
Hydrogen bonds in ice arrange molecules farther apart, making it less dense
Ice has fewer hydrogen bonds than liquid water
Ionic bonds hold ice in a compact lattice
questions.explanation: In ice, hydrogen bonds lock molecules into a spacious hexagonal lattice. This makes ice less dense than liquid water, so it floats — a fact critical for aquatic life in cold climates.

lessons.title: Biological Macromolecules
lessons.content:
Life is built from four classes of large organic molecules called `macromolecules`: carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids. Most are `polymers` — chains of repeating units called `monomers` — assembled by `dehydration synthesis` (releasing water) and broken apart by `hydrolysis` (adding water).

`Carbohydrates` store and deliver energy. Monosaccharides (glucose, fructose) are single-sugar monomers. Disaccharides (sucrose, lactose) are two joined monomers. Polysaccharides are long chains: `starch` stores energy in plants, `glycogen` in animals, and `cellulose` forms plant cell walls (humans cannot digest it).

`Lipids` are nonpolar and hydrophobic. Fats (triglycerides) store long-term energy. Phospholipids form cell membranes — their hydrophilic heads face water and their hydrophobic tails hide inside. Steroids (like cholesterol) have a four-ring structure and serve as hormones and membrane components.

`Proteins` are the most functionally diverse macromolecule. Monomers are `amino acids` (20 types), joined by `peptide bonds`. A protein's function depends entirely on its three-dimensional shape, which results from four levels of structure: primary (amino acid sequence), secondary (alpha helices / beta sheets from hydrogen bonding), tertiary (overall 3D folding), and quaternary (multiple polypeptide subunits). Functions include enzymes, structural support, transport, signaling, and immune defense.

`Nucleic acids` store and transmit genetic information. Monomers are `nucleotides` (sugar + phosphate + nitrogenous base). `DNA` uses the bases A, T, G, C and stores the genetic blueprint. `RNA` uses A, U, G, C and carries instructions from DNA to ribosomes.

examples.label: Monomer → Polymer Summary
examples.code:
Carbohydrates  monomer: monosaccharide   polymer: polysaccharide
Proteins       monomer: amino acid        polymer: polypeptide
Nucleic acids  monomer: nucleotide        polymer: DNA / RNA
Lipids         not true polymers (fats = glycerol + 3 fatty acids)

questions.q: A peptide bond forms between which two parts of adjacent amino acids?
options: An amino group and a carboxyl group
A phosphate group and a hydroxyl group
questions.explanation: A dehydration reaction links the amino group (–NH₂) of one amino acid to the carboxyl group (–COOH) of the next, releasing water and forming a peptide bond.

questions.q: Which level of protein structure refers to the specific sequence of amino acids?
questions.explanation: Primary structure is simply the linear sequence of amino acids, determined by the gene. All higher levels of structure follow from this sequence.

questions.q: Cellulose and starch are both polymers of glucose. Why can humans digest starch but not cellulose?
options: Cellulose monomers are fructose, which we lack enzymes for
The glycosidic linkages in cellulose are in a different orientation that human enzymes cannot break
Starch is soluble in water; cellulose is not
Cellulose contains nitrogen while starch does not
questions.explanation: Starch has alpha-1,4 glycosidic bonds; cellulose has beta-1,4 bonds. Human amylase only cleaves alpha linkages. The structural difference in bond orientation makes cellulose indigestible.

questions.prompt: Complete the statement about dehydration synthesis:
questions.code: When two monomers join, a molecule of ___ is released in a process called ___ synthesis.
questions.explanation: Dehydration synthesis removes water (H₂O) to form a covalent bond between monomers. The reverse, hydrolysis, adds water to break bonds.

questions.prompt: Fill in the missing macromolecule class:
questions.code:
Phospholipids are a type of ___, and their amphipathic nature
makes them ideal for forming cell ___.
questions.explanation: Phospholipids have hydrophilic phosphate heads and hydrophobic fatty acid tails, so they spontaneously form bilayers — the basis of all cell membranes.

questions.code:
DNA uses the base ___ where RNA uses ___.
RNA is typically ___ stranded.
questions.explanation: Thymine (T) in DNA is replaced by uracil (U) in RNA. Most RNA molecules are single-stranded, while DNA is a double helix.

lessons.title: Prokaryotic vs Eukaryotic Cells
lessons.content:
All life is made of cells. Cells fall into two fundamental categories based on their internal organization.

`Prokaryotic cells` lack a membrane-bound nucleus. Their DNA floats in the `cytoplasm` in a region called the nucleoid. They also lack membrane-bound organelles. Prokaryotes include all bacteria and archaea. They are generally smaller (1–10 µm) and simpler, but enormously successful — they colonize virtually every environment on Earth.

`Eukaryotic cells` have a true membrane-bound nucleus containing their DNA, as well as numerous membrane-bound organelles. Plants, animals, fungi, and protists are all eukaryotes. Animal cells range from 10–100 µm.

Both cell types share certain features: a `plasma membrane` (phospholipid bilayer controlling what enters and exits), `cytoplasm` (fluid interior), `ribosomes` (build proteins), and `DNA` as genetic material.

Key eukaryotic organelles: the `nucleus` stores DNA and directs cellular activity. The `endoplasmic reticulum` (ER) is a network of membranes — rough ER (studded with ribosomes) synthesizes proteins destined for export; smooth ER synthesizes lipids and detoxifies. The `Golgi apparatus` packages and ships proteins. `Mitochondria` produce ATP via cellular respiration. `Lysosomes` contain digestive enzymes. In plant cells: `chloroplasts` perform photosynthesis, and a rigid `cell wall` of cellulose provides structural support. A large central `vacuole` stores water and maintains turgor pressure.

examples.label: Quick Comparison
examples.code:
Feature          Prokaryote      Eukaryote
Nucleus          No              Yes
Organelles       No              Yes
DNA shape        Circular        Linear chromosomes
Size             ~1–10 µm        ~10–100 µm
Examples         Bacteria        Plants, Animals, Fungi

questions.q: A scientist discovers a new single-celled organism with a circular chromosome, no membrane-bound nucleus, and ribosomes smaller than those in human cells. This organism is most likely a:
questions.explanation: Circular chromosomes, absent membrane-bound nucleus, and 70S (smaller) ribosomes are all defining features of prokaryotes.

questions.q: Which organelle is responsible for modifying, sorting, and packaging proteins for secretion?
questions.explanation: The Golgi apparatus acts as the cell's postal system — it receives proteins from the rough ER, modifies them (e.g., adds sugar chains), then packages them into vesicles for delivery.

questions.q: A plant cell is placed in pure water. Water enters by osmosis, but the cell does not burst. What prevents this?
options: The plasma membrane actively pumps water out
The central vacuole contracts to expel excess water
The rigid cell wall exerts turgor pressure that counteracts osmotic pressure
Chloroplasts consume the incoming water immediately
questions.explanation: The cellulose cell wall is rigid. As water enters, it generates turgor pressure against the wall. The wall pushes back, reaching equilibrium without the cell lysing.

lessons.title: Cell Membrane & Transport
lessons.content:
The `plasma membrane` is a fluid phospholipid bilayer embedded with proteins. It is selectively permeable — small nonpolar molecules (O₂, CO₂) pass freely; large or charged molecules need help.

`Passive transport` moves substances down their concentration gradient (high → low) with no energy cost. `Diffusion` is the net movement of any molecule from high to low concentration. `Osmosis` is the diffusion of water across a semipermeable membrane from low solute (high water) concentration to high solute (low water) concentration.

A solution is `isotonic` if its solute concentration equals the cell's — no net water movement. `Hypotonic` solution has less solute than the cell — water enters (cells swell or lyse). `Hypertonic` solution has more solute — water exits (cells shrink/crenate).

`Facilitated diffusion` uses channel or carrier proteins to move polar/large molecules down their gradient — still passive, no ATP needed.

`Active transport` moves substances against their concentration gradient (low → high) and requires ATP. The `sodium-potassium pump` is a classic example: it uses ATP to move 3 Na⁺ out and 2 K⁺ in per cycle, maintaining electrochemical gradients vital for nerve and muscle function.

`Bulk transport`: `Endocytosis` engulfs material into the cell (phagocytosis = solids, pinocytosis = fluids). `Exocytosis` fuses vesicles with the membrane to release material outside.

questions.q: Red blood cells placed in a hypertonic solution will:
options: Shrink (crenate) as water leaves by osmosis
Actively pump water out using ATP
questions.explanation: In a hypertonic solution, solute concentration outside exceeds that inside the cell. Water moves out by osmosis, causing the cell to shrink — a process called crenation.

questions.q: The sodium-potassium pump moves ions against their concentration gradient. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Moving ions against their gradient requires energy input (ATP). Active transport is defined by movement against the concentration gradient using cellular energy.

questions.q: Which of the following can cross a phospholipid bilayer without any protein assistance?
questions.explanation: CO₂ is small and nonpolar — it dissolves directly in the hydrophobic core of the bilayer and crosses freely. Glucose, ions, and DNA all need transport proteins or other mechanisms.

lessons.title: Spot the Bug: Membrane Transport
lessons.prompt: A student wrote a summary of membrane transport. One line contains a factual error. Find it.
lessons.code:
1. The plasma membrane is a phospholipid bilayer.
2. Passive transport requires no ATP and moves molecules down their gradient.
3. Osmosis is the diffusion of water toward lower solute concentration.
4. The sodium-potassium pump moves 3 Na+ out and 2 K+ in per ATP used.
5. Endocytosis is the process of releasing vesicle contents outside the cell.
lessons.explanation: Line 5 describes exocytosis, not endocytosis. Endocytosis brings material into the cell by engulfing it in a vesicle. Exocytosis releases vesicle contents to the exterior.

lessons.title: Enzymes & Metabolism
lessons.content:
All chemical reactions in a cell constitute its `metabolism`. Reactions that build complex molecules from simpler ones are `anabolic` (require energy). Reactions that break down complex molecules are `catabolic` (release energy). The energy currency of cells is `ATP` (adenosine triphosphate), whose hydrolysis releases energy to power cellular work.

`Enzymes` are biological catalysts — almost all are proteins. They lower the `activation energy` required to start a reaction, dramatically speeding up reaction rates without being consumed. Each enzyme has an `active site`, a specifically shaped region where the substrate binds. The fit between enzyme and substrate is described by the `induced fit model` — binding causes a conformational change that improves the fit and strains the substrate's bonds.

Enzyme activity is affected by temperature (too hot = denaturation destroys shape), pH (each enzyme has an optimal pH), and substrate concentration. `Enzyme inhibitors` reduce activity: `competitive inhibitors` block the active site; `noncompetitive inhibitors` bind elsewhere and change the enzyme's shape. Many inhibitors are reversible. Irreversible inhibitors permanently disable enzymes (e.g., nerve agents blocking acetylcholinesterase).

`Cofactors` are inorganic ions (e.g., Mg²⁺, Zn²⁺) required by some enzymes. `Coenzymes` are organic cofactors (e.g., NAD⁺, FAD) that shuttle electrons between reactions.

questions.q: An inhibitor binds to a site other than the active site and changes the enzyme's shape so the substrate can no longer bind. This is:
questions.explanation: Noncompetitive inhibitors bind an allosteric site, inducing a conformational change that distorts the active site. This reduces activity even when substrate is abundant.

questions.q: Raising temperature from 37°C to 80°C would most likely:
options: Increase enzyme activity by providing more kinetic energy to substrates
Denature the enzyme by disrupting its three-dimensional shape
Increase substrate affinity by tightening the active site
Have no effect because enzymes are not affected by heat
questions.explanation: Extreme heat disrupts the hydrogen bonds and other interactions that maintain an enzyme's tertiary structure. Denaturation unfolds the protein, destroying the active site.

questions.q: Which statement about enzymes after a reaction is correct?
options: They are consumed and must be synthesized again
They become part of the product molecule
They are released unchanged and can catalyze additional reactions
They permanently bond to the substrate
questions.explanation: Enzymes are catalysts — they are not consumed. After the product is released, the active site is free to bind another substrate molecule, repeating the cycle.

lessons.title: Cellular Respiration
lessons.content:
Cellular respiration is the process by which cells extract energy from organic molecules, primarily glucose, and store it as ATP. The overall equation is:

`C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP`

It occurs in three main stages:

`1. Glycolysis` — occurs in the cytoplasm, requires no oxygen. Glucose (6C) is split into two pyruvate molecules (3C each). Net yield: 2 ATP and 2 NADH.

`2. Pyruvate Oxidation & Krebs Cycle` — occurs in the mitochondrial matrix. Pyruvate is converted to acetyl-CoA, releasing CO₂. Acetyl-CoA enters the Krebs cycle, which generates CO₂, NADH, FADH₂, and a small amount of ATP per turn. The cycle turns twice per glucose.

`3. Oxidative Phosphorylation` — occurs at the inner mitochondrial membrane. NADH and FADH₂ donate electrons to the `electron transport chain` (ETC). Electrons move through protein complexes, pumping H⁺ ions into the intermembrane space. H⁺ flow back through `ATP synthase` (chemiosmosis) drives ATP production. O₂ is the final electron acceptor, combining with H⁺ to form water. This stage produces ~32–34 ATP per glucose.

`Anaerobic respiration / fermentation` occurs when O₂ is absent. Glycolysis still runs, but pyruvate is converted to lactate (in animals) or ethanol + CO₂ (in yeast) to regenerate NAD⁺ so glycolysis can continue. Net yield: only 2 ATP per glucose.

examples.label: ATP Yield Summary
examples.code:
Stage                    Location             ATP yield (approx)
Glycolysis               Cytoplasm            2 ATP, 2 NADH
Pyruvate oxidation       Mito. matrix         2 NADH (no ATP directly)
Krebs cycle              Mito. matrix         2 ATP, 6 NADH, 2 FADH₂
Oxidative phosphorylation Inner mito. membrane ~28–32 ATP
Total                                         ~30–36 ATP per glucose

questions.q: Where does glycolysis take place, and what is its net ATP yield per glucose?
questions.explanation: Glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm and does not require oxygen. It produces 4 ATP but invests 2, for a net gain of 2 ATP, plus 2 NADH.

questions.q: What is the role of oxygen in aerobic respiration?
options: It is the starting reactant for glycolysis
It drives ATP synthase directly by binding to it
It serves as the final electron acceptor in the electron transport chain, forming water
It regenerates NAD⁺ in the cytoplasm
questions.explanation: O₂ accepts the electrons at the end of the ETC and combines with H⁺ to form water. Without O₂, electrons back up, the ETC halts, and oxidative phosphorylation stops.

questions.q: During vigorous exercise, human muscle cells switch to lactic acid fermentation. The PRIMARY reason is to:
options: Produce more ATP than aerobic respiration
Regenerate NAD⁺ so glycolysis can continue producing ATP
Break down lactic acid into CO₂ and water
questions.explanation: Fermentation does not produce extra ATP. Its sole purpose is to oxidize NADH back to NAD⁺, which is required to keep glycolysis running when the ETC is oxygen-limited.

lessons.title: Photosynthesis
lessons.content:
Photosynthesis converts light energy into chemical energy stored in glucose. It occurs in `chloroplasts` and follows the overall equation:

`6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂`

Chloroplasts have a double membrane; inside are stacked thylakoid membranes (`grana`) surrounded by the fluid `stroma`. Photosynthesis has two stages:

`Light-dependent reactions` occur in the thylakoid membranes. Chlorophyll absorbs light (primarily red and blue; reflects green). Water is split (`photolysis`), releasing O₂ as a byproduct. Light energy drives the ETC to pump H⁺, producing ATP via `chemiosmosis`. NADP⁺ is reduced to NADPH. Products: ATP, NADPH, O₂.

`Calvin Cycle` (light-independent / dark reactions) occurs in the stroma. CO₂ is `fixed` by the enzyme `RuBisCO` onto a 5-carbon compound (RuBP), producing an unstable 6C molecule that splits into two 3C molecules (3-PGA). ATP and NADPH from the light reactions are used to reduce 3-PGA into G3P (glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate), which can be used to build glucose. RuBP is regenerated. Three turns of the cycle fix 3 CO₂ and produce one net G3P.

Chlorophyll is found in `photosystems I and II`. Photosystem II (P680) acts first — it splits water. Photosystem I (P700) re-energizes electrons to make NADPH.

examples.label: Inputs and Outputs
examples.code:
Light reactions:  Light + H₂O  →  ATP + NADPH + O₂
Calvin cycle:     CO₂ + ATP + NADPH  →  G3P (then glucose)

Remember: O₂ comes from WATER splitting, not CO₂.

questions.q: The oxygen released during photosynthesis comes from:
options: Water molecules split during the light reactions
RuBP being oxidized in the Calvin cycle
questions.explanation: Photolysis splits H₂O → 2H⁺ + ½O₂ + 2e⁻. The electrons replace those lost by chlorophyll, and O₂ is released as a byproduct — not from CO₂.

questions.q: What would happen to the Calvin cycle if the light reactions were blocked?
options: It would continue using stored CO₂
It would run in reverse, producing CO₂ from glucose
It would stop because it depends on ATP and NADPH from the light reactions
It would switch to using oxygen as an energy source
questions.explanation: The Calvin cycle requires ATP and NADPH produced by the light reactions to fix CO₂ and reduce 3-PGA into G3P. Without those inputs, the cycle halts.

questions.q: RuBisCO is the enzyme that catalyzes carbon fixation. It combines CO₂ with:
questions.explanation: RuBisCO adds CO₂ to RuBP (a 5-carbon compound), forming an unstable 6C intermediate that immediately splits into two 3-PGA molecules.

lessons.title: ATP: Energy Currency of the Cell
lessons.prompt: Write pseudocode or a brief outline showing the flow of energy in a cell — starting from glucose, through cellular respiration, to doing cellular work. Include the three main stages and where ATP is used. Minimum: name all three stages and both locations (cytoplasm / mitochondria). Format each stage on its own line.

validation.message: Must mention the Krebs cycle (or citric acid cycle).
Must mention oxidative phosphorylation or the electron transport chain.
Must mention the cytoplasm (location of glycolysis).

lessons.solution:
Step 1 — Glycolysis (cytoplasm):
  Glucose → 2 Pyruvate
  Net: 2 ATP, 2 NADH

Step 2 — Krebs Cycle (mitochondrial matrix):
  Pyruvate → Acetyl-CoA → CO₂
  Produces: NADH, FADH₂, 2 ATP

Step 3 — Oxidative Phosphorylation (inner mitochondrial membrane):
  NADH + FADH₂ → ETC → proton gradient → ATP synthase
  O₂ accepts electrons → H₂O
  Produces: ~32 ATP

Total: ~36 ATP used for muscle contraction, active transport, biosynthesis

lessons.title: The Cell Cycle & Mitosis
lessons.content:
Cells reproduce by dividing. The `cell cycle` is the ordered sequence of events from one cell division to the next. It has two broad phases:

`Interphase` (90% of the cycle): the cell grows and prepares for division. It consists of G₁ (cell growth, normal functions), S phase (DNA replication — each chromosome is duplicated into two sister chromatids joined at the centromere), and G₂ (further growth, preparation for mitosis).

`Mitosis` (M phase) divides the nucleus so each daughter cell gets a complete, identical set of chromosomes. It has four stages:
1. `Prophase` — chromatin condenses into visible chromosomes; the mitotic spindle begins forming; the nuclear envelope breaks down.
2. `Metaphase` — chromosomes align at the cell's equator (metaphase plate); spindle fibers attach to centromeres.
3. `Anaphase` — sister chromatids are pulled to opposite poles of the cell.
4. `Telophase` — nuclear envelopes reform around each set of chromosomes; chromosomes decondense.

`Cytokinesis` then divides the cytoplasm: animal cells form a `cleavage furrow`; plant cells build a `cell plate` that becomes the new cell wall.

The cell cycle is regulated by `checkpoints` (G₁, G₂, and M checkpoints) that verify DNA integrity and cell size. Key regulators include `cyclins` and `cyclin-dependent kinases (CDKs)`. Loss of checkpoint control leads to `cancer` — uncontrolled cell division.

questions.q: During which phase of the cell cycle does DNA replication occur?
questions.explanation: DNA synthesis (replication) occurs specifically during S phase of interphase, producing sister chromatids joined at centromeres before mitosis begins.

questions.q: At the metaphase-to-anaphase transition, what event occurs?
options: Chromosomes condense for the first time
Sister chromatids are separated and pulled to opposite poles
questions.explanation: At the metaphase-anaphase transition, cohesin proteins holding sister chromatids together are cleaved, and spindle fibers shorten to pull each chromatid to an opposite pole.

questions.q: A cell has 46 chromosomes before mitosis. How many chromosomes does each daughter cell have after mitosis and cytokinesis?
questions.explanation: Mitosis produces two genetically identical daughter cells. DNA was replicated in S phase, and mitosis distributes one copy of each chromosome to each cell, maintaining the count at 46.

lessons.title: Meiosis & Sexual Reproduction
lessons.content:
Sexual reproduction requires the production of `gametes` (sperm and eggs) with half the normal chromosome number. `Meiosis` is the specialized division that produces four genetically unique haploid (n) cells from one diploid (2n) parent cell.

Meiosis consists of two divisions:

`Meiosis I` (`reductive division`): Homologous chromosome pairs (one from each parent) line up together. During `prophase I`, homologs pair up (synapsis) and exchange segments in a process called `crossing over` (recombination) — this shuffles alleles and is a major source of genetic variation. In `anaphase I`, homologous chromosomes (not sister chromatids) separate. Each resulting cell has half the chromosome number but chromosomes still consist of two sister chromatids.

`Meiosis II` resembles mitosis: sister chromatids separate, producing four haploid cells. In males all four become sperm; in females one becomes an egg and three become non-functional polar bodies.

Genetic variation arises from: (1) `crossing over` during prophase I, (2) `independent assortment` — each pair of homologs aligns randomly, creating 2²³ possible chromosome combinations in humans, and (3) `random fertilization`.

Comparison: Mitosis → 2 diploid identical cells (growth, repair). Meiosis → 4 haploid genetically unique cells (sexual reproduction).

questions.q: Crossing over during prophase I of meiosis contributes to genetic diversity by:
options: Doubling the number of chromosomes in daughter cells
Exchanging segments between homologous chromosomes, creating new allele combinations
Separating sister chromatids earlier than normal
questions.explanation: Crossing over occurs between non-sister chromatids of homologous pairs. The exchange creates chromosomes with new combinations of alleles not present in either original chromosome.

questions.q: A human diploid cell (2n = 46) undergoes meiosis. The resulting gametes each have:
questions.explanation: Meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes, halving the number. Each gamete has 23 chromosomes — one from each homologous pair.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a source of genetic variation in meiosis?
options: DNA replication in S phase before meiosis I
questions.explanation: DNA replication before meiosis creates identical copies (sister chromatids) — it does not introduce variation. Variation comes from crossing over, independent assortment, and random fertilization.

questions.prompt: Fill in the blanks about the cell cycle:
questions.code:
DNA replication occurs in ___ phase.
Mitosis produces ___ daughter cells, each ___.
questions.explanation: S phase (synthesis) is when DNA is replicated. Mitosis produces two diploid (2n) cells genetically identical to the parent.

questions.code:
In meiosis I, ___ chromosomes separate.
In meiosis II, ___ chromatids separate.
The final result is ___ haploid cells.
questions.explanation: Meiosis I separates homologous pairs (reductive division). Meiosis II separates sister chromatids, like mitosis. Four haploid cells result.

lessons.title: Mendelian Genetics
lessons.content:
Gregor Mendel's experiments with pea plants in the 1860s established the foundational rules of inheritance, even before genes or chromosomes were understood.

`Law of Segregation`: Each organism has two alleles for each trait. During gamete formation, these alleles separate so each gamete carries only one allele. An organism's two alleles may be identical (`homozygous`) or different (`heterozygous`). The expressed physical trait is the `phenotype`; the genetic makeup is the `genotype`. A `dominant` allele masks a `recessive` allele when both are present (Aa = dominant phenotype). Only aa shows the recessive phenotype.

Punnett squares predict the probability of offspring genotypes. A cross of two heterozygotes (Aa × Aa) produces a 3:1 phenotype ratio (3 dominant : 1 recessive) and a 1:2:1 genotype ratio (AA : Aa : aa).

`Law of Independent Assortment`: Genes on different chromosomes are inherited independently (one gene's alleles don't influence another's segregation). A dihybrid cross (AaBb × AaBb) yields a 9:3:3:1 phenotype ratio.

Beyond simple dominance: `Incomplete dominance` — heterozygote has an intermediate phenotype (red × white = pink). `Codominance` — both alleles are fully expressed (AB blood type). `Multiple alleles` — ABO blood type has three alleles (Iᴬ, Iᴮ, i). `Polygenic inheritance` — multiple genes affect one trait (skin color, height), producing a continuous distribution.

examples.label: Monohybrid Cross: Tt × Tt
examples.code:
T       t
T   TT      Tt
t   Tt      tt

Genotype ratio: 1 TT : 2 Tt : 1 tt
Phenotype ratio: 3 tall : 1 short

questions.q: Two parents who are both carriers of a recessive disease (Aa × Aa) have a child. What is the probability the child will be affected (aa)?
questions.explanation: Aa × Aa produces: ¼ AA, ½ Aa, ¼ aa. Only aa expresses the recessive disease, giving a 25% (1 in 4) probability.

questions.q: In a cross between a red-flowered plant and a white-flowered plant, all offspring are pink. This demonstrates:
questions.explanation: Incomplete dominance produces an intermediate phenotype in heterozygotes. Neither allele fully masks the other, so the result is a blend (pink).

questions.q: Mendel's Law of Independent Assortment applies when genes are located on:
options: Different chromosomes or far apart on the same chromosome
questions.explanation: Independent assortment holds when genes are on different (non-homologous) chromosomes or far enough apart that crossing over makes them behave independently.

lessons.title: DNA Structure & Replication
lessons.content:
DNA (`deoxyribonucleic acid`) stores genetic information. Its structure, revealed by Watson and Crick in 1953 using Rosalind Franklin's X-ray data, is a `double helix` — two antiparallel strands wound around each other.

Each strand is a polymer of `nucleotides`, each containing: deoxyribose sugar, a phosphate group, and a nitrogenous base (A, T, G, or C). The two strands are held together by `hydrogen bonds` between complementary base pairs: `A pairs with T` (2 hydrogen bonds), `G pairs with C` (3 hydrogen bonds). The two strands run antiparallel (5'→3' and 3'→5').

`DNA replication` is `semiconservative` — each new double helix consists of one original strand and one new strand. Key steps:
1. `Helicase` unwinds and separates the double helix at the origin of replication, forming a replication fork.
2. `Primase` adds a short RNA primer to provide a 3'-OH starting point.
3. `DNA polymerase III` reads the template 3'→5' and synthesizes the new strand 5'→3', adding nucleotides via complementary base pairing.
4. The `leading strand` is synthesized continuously toward the fork. The `lagging strand` is synthesized in fragments (`Okazaki fragments`) away from the fork.
5. RNA primers are replaced with DNA; `DNA ligase` joins Okazaki fragments.
6. `DNA polymerase I` has proofreading ability, reducing errors to ~1 per 10⁹ base pairs.

questions.q: DNA replication is described as 'semiconservative' because:
options: Only half of the DNA molecule is replicated at each cell division
Each new double helix contains one original strand and one newly synthesized strand
Replication uses half the cell's energy reserves
Half the nucleotides come from the diet
questions.explanation: Meselson and Stahl's classic experiment confirmed this: the two original strands separate, each serving as a template. Each daughter molecule has one parental and one new strand.

questions.q: Why must DNA polymerase have a primer?
options: Primers provide the energy needed for polymerization
DNA polymerase can only add nucleotides to an existing 3'-OH group, not start a new strand
Primers prevent helicase from re-annealing the strands
Without primers, DNA polymerase would replicate RNA instead
questions.explanation: DNA polymerase cannot initiate a new strand — it can only extend an existing one. Primase synthesizes a short RNA primer with a free 3'-OH that DNA polymerase then extends.

questions.q: If one strand of a DNA molecule has the sequence 5'-ATCGGC-3', what is the complementary strand?
questions.explanation: Complementary base pairing (A-T, G-C) and antiparallel orientation: 5'-ATCGGC-3' pairs with 3'-TAGCCG-5', which written 5'→3' is 5'-GCCGAT-3'.

lessons.title: Gene Expression: Transcription & Translation
lessons.content:
The `central dogma` of molecular biology describes how genetic information flows: `DNA → RNA → Protein`.

`Transcription` — DNA is copied into mRNA in the nucleus:
1. `RNA polymerase` binds to the `promoter` region, a specific DNA sequence marking the gene's start.
2. The DNA helix unwinds, and RNA polymerase reads the template strand 3'→5', building an mRNA strand 5'→3' using complementary base pairing (A-U, T-A, G-C, C-G).
3. The pre-mRNA is processed: a `5' cap` and `poly-A tail` are added for stability; `introns` (non-coding sequences) are removed by spliceosomes, leaving `exons` joined together.
4. Mature mRNA exits the nucleus through nuclear pores.

`Translation` — mRNA is decoded by ribosomes into a protein sequence:
1. The ribosome assembles at the `start codon` (AUG, codes for methionine).
2. `tRNA` molecules bring amino acids. Each tRNA has an `anticodon` complementary to an mRNA `codon` (3-base sequence).
3. The ribosome moves along the mRNA 5'→3', adding amino acids in sequence.
4. Translation stops at a `stop codon` (UAA, UAG, or UGA) — no tRNA matches these.
5. The polypeptide is released and folds into its functional shape.

The `genetic code` is nearly universal (same codons in bacteria, plants, humans) and `redundant` (multiple codons can specify the same amino acid).

examples.label: From DNA to Protein
examples.code:
DNA template:  3'-TACGGGCTA-5'
mRNA:          5'-AUGCCCGAU-3'
tRNA anticodon:   UAC GGG CUA
Amino acids:   Met - Pro - Asp

AUG = start codon (always Met)

questions.q: During transcription, RNA polymerase reads the template strand in which direction?
options: Either direction depending on the promoter
questions.explanation: RNA polymerase reads the DNA template strand 3'→5', synthesizing the mRNA in the 5'→3' direction. This is the same rule as DNA polymerase.

questions.q: A mutation changes the mRNA codon UCA (serine) to UCG (also serine). What type of mutation is this?
questions.explanation: A silent mutation changes a codon but not the amino acid, because the genetic code is redundant. UCA and UCG both code for serine, so the protein is unaffected.

questions.q: What is the function of tRNA in translation?
options: It carries the genetic information from the nucleus to the ribosome
It brings specific amino acids to the ribosome and matches them to mRNA codons via its anticodon
It processes introns out of pre-mRNA
questions.explanation: tRNA acts as an adaptor — one end carries a specific amino acid, the other has an anticodon that base-pairs with the complementary codon on mRNA, ensuring the correct amino acid is added.

lessons.title: Spot the Bug: Gene Expression
lessons.prompt: A student's notes on gene expression contain one factual error. Identify the incorrect line.
lessons.code:
1. DNA is transcribed into mRNA in the nucleus.
2. RNA polymerase binds to the promoter to begin transcription.
3. Introns are the coding sequences kept in mature mRNA.
4. Translation occurs at ribosomes in the cytoplasm.
5. A stop codon ends translation; no amino acid is added.
lessons.explanation: Introns are the NON-coding sequences that are spliced OUT of pre-mRNA. Exons are the coding sequences that are retained and joined together to form mature mRNA. The student has them reversed.

lessons.title: Natural Selection & Evolutionary Theory
lessons.content:
Charles Darwin proposed `natural selection` as the mechanism of evolution after observing variation in populations on the Galápagos Islands. Alfred Russel Wallace independently developed the same idea.

`Evolution` is defined as change in the allele frequencies of a population over time. Natural selection requires four conditions:
1. `Variation` — individuals in a population differ in heritable traits.
2. `Heritability` — traits are passed from parent to offspring.
3. `Overproduction` — more offspring are born than can survive.
4. `Differential survival and reproduction` — individuals with advantageous traits are more likely to survive and reproduce.

Over generations, advantageous alleles increase in frequency in the population. `Fitness` is measured as reproductive success, not physical strength.

Types of natural selection based on effect on phenotype distribution:
- `Directional selection` — one extreme phenotype is favored; population shifts toward it.
- `Stabilizing selection` — intermediate phenotypes are favored; variation decreases (e.g., human birth weight).
- `Disruptive selection` — both extremes are favored over the intermediate; may drive speciation.

Other evolutionary mechanisms: `Genetic drift` — random allele frequency changes, especially in small populations (`bottleneck effect`, `founder effect`). `Gene flow` — movement of alleles between populations. `Mutation` — ultimate source of new alleles. `Sexual selection` — selection based on mate choice (peacock's tail).

The `Hardy-Weinberg principle` states that allele frequencies remain constant in a population that is: large, randomly mating, with no mutation, migration, or selection. It serves as a null model for detecting evolution.

questions.q: In a cheetah population that passed through a near-extinction event (very few survivors), researchers find very low genetic diversity. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: The bottleneck effect occurs when a population is drastically reduced. The survivors are a random, unrepresentative sample, so genetic diversity is lost. The founder effect applies when a small group colonizes a new area.

questions.q: Natural selection acts on phenotypes, but evolution is measured as changes in:
options: Allele frequencies in a population over generations
The number of species in an ecosystem
questions.explanation: Evolution is a population-level phenomenon. Natural selection acts on phenotypes, but the heritable basis (alleles) is what changes in frequency over generations — that is evolution.

questions.q: Human birth weight shows stabilizing selection — very small and very large babies have lower survival rates. This means:
options: The population is evolving toward heavier babies
Intermediate birth weights are most fit; variation around the mean decreases
Both extremes of birth weight are being selected for simultaneously
Birth weight is not heritable and cannot be selected
questions.explanation: Stabilizing selection favors the intermediate phenotype and reduces variation. It maintains the current norm rather than shifting the population toward either extreme.

lessons.title: Speciation & Phylogenetics
lessons.content:
A `species` is most commonly defined (biological species concept) as a group of populations whose members can interbreed and produce fertile offspring, but are reproductively isolated from other groups.

`Speciation` is the formation of new species, requiring the evolution of `reproductive isolation` — barriers that prevent interbreeding. `Prezygotic barriers` prevent mating or fertilization: habitat isolation, temporal isolation (breed at different times), behavioral isolation, mechanical isolation, and gametic isolation. `Postzygotic barriers` act after fertilization: hybrid inviability, hybrid sterility (mules — horse × donkey), and hybrid breakdown.

`Allopatric speciation` — geographic separation divides a population. Isolated populations diverge genetically through mutation, drift, and selection. If they come back into contact and can no longer interbreed, new species have formed. This is the most common mode.

`Sympatric speciation` — new species arise within the same geographic area. Polyploidy (extra sets of chromosomes) is the most common mechanism in plants — a polyploid individual cannot mate with diploid parents.

`Phylogenetics` studies evolutionary relationships. A `phylogenetic tree` (cladogram) shows shared ancestry. `Homologous structures` (similar anatomy from a common ancestor, e.g., human arm / whale flipper / bat wing) provide evidence. `Analogous structures` (similar function but different ancestry, e.g., bird wing / insect wing) result from `convergent evolution`. `Vestigial structures` (reduced/non-functional remnants, e.g., human coccyx) support common descent.

questions.q: Horses and donkeys can mate to produce mules, but mules are sterile. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: A postzygotic barrier acts after fertilization. Hybrid sterility — offspring form but are infertile — prevents gene flow between species even when mating occurs.

questions.q: The forelimbs of humans, whales, and bats have the same underlying bone structure but serve very different functions. These are:
options: Vestigial structures with no current function
questions.explanation: Homologous structures share an evolutionary origin (common ancestor) even if their current functions differ. The same bones (humerus, radius, ulna, carpals) appear across these species.

questions.q: A plant species undergoes an error in meiosis, producing gametes with double the normal chromosome number. Self-fertilization produces a polyploid offspring. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Polyploidy can instantly create a reproductively isolated individual within the same geographic area — true sympatric speciation. This is common in plants (wheat is a hexaploid).

questions.code:
For allele frequencies to remain constant, a population must be
___, have ___ mating, and have no mutation,
___, or natural selection.
questions.explanation: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium requires: large population, random mating, no mutation, no migration (gene flow), and no natural selection. Violation of any condition means evolution is occurring.

questions.code:
___ selection favors extreme phenotypes at both ends.
___ selection narrows variation around the mean.
___ selection shifts the population toward one extreme.
questions.explanation: Disruptive → both extremes; Stabilizing → intermediate; Directional → one extreme. Each has distinct effects on population variation.

lessons.title: Populations & Growth
lessons.content:
Ecology studies interactions between organisms and their environment. It is organized into levels: individual → population → community → ecosystem → biosphere.

A `population` is a group of individuals of the same species in the same area. Key properties:
- `Population size (N)` — total number of individuals.
- `Density` — individuals per unit area or volume.
- `Dispersion` — spatial pattern: clumped (most common — resources patchy), uniform (e.g., territorial animals), or random.

`Population growth models`:
`Exponential growth` (J-curve): occurs when resources are unlimited. Growth rate is proportional to population size. Bacteria in ideal conditions follow this pattern. Formula: dN/dt = rN, where r is the intrinsic growth rate.

`Logistic growth` (S-curve): occurs when resources are limited. Growth slows as population approaches the `carrying capacity (K)` — the maximum population size the environment can sustain. Formula: dN/dt = rN[(K-N)/K]. When N = K, growth stops.

`Limiting factors` determine carrying capacity: food, water, space, disease, predation. `Density-dependent factors` intensify as population grows (competition, predation, disease). `Density-independent factors` are unaffected by density (weather, natural disasters).

Life history strategies: `r-strategists` have high reproduction rates, small offspring, little parental care — thrive in variable environments (insects, annual plants). `K-strategists` have low reproduction, large offspring, extensive parental care — thrive in stable environments near K (elephants, humans).

questions.q: A bacterial population doubles every 20 minutes in ideal conditions. This follows which growth model?
options: Exponential growth with no limiting factors
Logistic growth with a high carrying capacity
questions.explanation: Exponential growth (J-curve) occurs when resources are unlimited and birth rate far exceeds death rate. Bacterial cultures in ideal lab conditions are the classic example.

questions.q: As a population approaches its carrying capacity, the logistic growth model predicts that the growth rate will:
options: Accelerate because more individuals are reproducing
Decrease and approach zero as N approaches K
Drop to negative values, crashing the population
questions.explanation: In logistic growth, the term [(K–N)/K] decreases as N approaches K, slowing the growth rate. At N = K, growth = 0. The population stabilizes.

questions.q: A disease that kills a greater percentage of individuals in a dense population than in a sparse one is an example of a:
questions.explanation: Density-dependent factors (disease, predation, competition) have a greater per-capita effect as population density increases, acting as a negative feedback on population growth.

lessons.title: Communities, Food Webs & Ecosystems
lessons.content:
A `community` is all the interacting species in an area. A `trophic level` describes an organism's position in a food chain based on its energy source.

`Producers` (autotrophs) capture energy from the sun (photosynthesis) or chemicals (chemosynthesis). `Primary consumers` eat producers. `Secondary consumers` eat primary consumers. `Tertiary consumers` eat secondary consumers. `Decomposers` (bacteria, fungi) break down dead organic matter.

A `food web` shows the complex interconnections of who eats whom. Energy flows through trophic levels, but with significant loss: roughly 10% of energy is transferred between levels (the `10% rule`). The rest is lost as heat (metabolism) or not consumed. This limits food chain length and explains why more biomass exists at lower trophic levels — shown as an `energy pyramid`.

Species interactions: `Predation` (+/–), `Competition` (–/–) — can lead to competitive exclusion (one species eliminates another) or resource partitioning (species divide resources to coexist). `Mutualism` (+/+, e.g., mycorrhizae — fungi and plant roots). `Commensalism` (+/0, e.g., barnacles on whales). `Parasitism` (+/–). `Keystone species` have a disproportionately large effect on community structure relative to their abundance (sea otters controlling sea urchin populations).

`Ecological succession`: `Primary succession` — life colonizes bare rock (lichens first, then mosses, shrubs, trees). `Secondary succession` — recovery after disturbance (forest fire), starting from soil — faster because soil and seed bank remain.

`Biogeochemical cycles` recycle matter through ecosystems: the `carbon cycle` (photosynthesis, respiration, decomposition, combustion), `nitrogen cycle` (N₂ fixation by bacteria → ammonia → nitrates → plant uptake → denitrification), and `water cycle`.

questions.q: If a grassland contains 10,000 kg of grass biomass, how much energy (biomass) would you expect at the level of secondary consumers?
questions.explanation: Applying the 10% rule twice: 10,000 kg grass → 1,000 kg primary consumers → 100 kg secondary consumers. Each transfer retains ~10% of energy.

questions.q: Sea otters eat sea urchins, which eat kelp. When sea otters were hunted to near extinction, sea urchin populations exploded and kelp forests collapsed. Sea otters are an example of a:
questions.explanation: A keystone species has an outsized effect on ecosystem structure relative to its abundance. Sea otters' removal caused a cascade of changes far beyond their direct prey.

questions.q: After a volcanic eruption covers an area with bare lava, the first colonizers are typically:
options: Large animals that return from neighboring areas
questions.explanation: Primary succession begins on bare substrate with no soil. Pioneer species like lichens (photosynthetic algae + fungi in mutualism) break down rock and add organic matter, enabling later species.

lessons.title: Build a Food Web
lessons.prompt: Design a simple terrestrial food web with at least 5 organisms spanning at least 3 trophic levels. Label each organism's trophic level. Include at least one example of a specific species interaction (predation, competition, mutualism, parasitism, or commensalism) and name it. Write each organism on its own line with arrows (→) showing energy flow direction (eaten → eater).

validation.message: Use arrows (→) to show energy flow between organisms.
Include at least one producer (plant, algae, etc.) at the base.
Label trophic levels (primary consumer, secondary consumer, etc.).
Name at least one specific species interaction.

lessons.solution:
Trophic Levels:
  Producer:          Grass
  Primary consumer:  Grasshopper → Grass
  Secondary consumer: Frog → Grasshopper
  Tertiary consumer: Snake → Frog
  Quaternary consumer: Hawk → Snake

Energy flow: Grass → Grasshopper → Frog → Snake → Hawk

Interaction example:
  Predation (+/–): Hawk preys on Snake
  Mutualism (+/+): Grass root and mycorrhizal fungi exchange nutrients

Decomposers: Bacteria and fungi break down all dead organisms,
returning nutrients to the soil (supports producers).

lessons.title: Biomes & Global Climate
lessons.content:
A `biome` is a large-scale community defined by its dominant vegetation and climate. Climate — primarily temperature and precipitation — determines which biome exists in a region.

Major terrestrial biomes:
- `Tropical rainforest` — near equator; warm, wet year-round; highest biodiversity; nutrient-poor soil (nutrients in biomass).
- `Tropical savanna` — seasonal rainfall; grasses dominate; large herbivores.
- `Desert` — less than 25 cm precipitation/year; extreme temperatures; xerophyte adaptations (cacti — store water; CAM photosynthesis).
- `Temperate grassland (prairie)` — seasonal, little rain; fertile soil; bison, prairie dogs.
- `Temperate deciduous forest` — moderate rainfall, four seasons; trees lose leaves in winter (maple, oak).
- `Boreal forest (taiga)` — cold, moderate precipitation; conifers (spruce, fir); long winters.
- `Tundra` — extreme cold, low precipitation; permafrost; dwarf shrubs, mosses; low productivity.

`Aquatic biomes` cover ~75% of Earth: freshwater (rivers, lakes, wetlands) and marine (ocean, estuary, coral reef — highest marine biodiversity, kelp forests, intertidal zones). `Estuaries` — where rivers meet the sea — are among the most productive ecosystems due to nutrient input.

`Climate change`: Burning fossil fuels increases atmospheric CO₂, enhancing the `greenhouse effect` and raising global temperatures. Consequences include polar ice melt, rising sea levels, shifted biome boundaries, altered precipitation patterns, and ocean acidification (CO₂ + H₂O → carbonic acid), which threatens coral reefs and shell-forming organisms.

questions.q: Tropical rainforests have extremely high biodiversity but nutrient-poor soil. Why?
options: Heavy rain leaches nutrients from soil and decomposition returns them directly to living biomass
High temperatures prevent decomposition, so nutrients accumulate in soil
Plants in rainforests do not require soil nutrients
The soil is too acidic for nutrients to form
questions.explanation: In tropical rainforests, heavy rainfall leaches nutrients out of the soil rapidly. Decomposition is fast in the warm, wet conditions, and nutrients are immediately taken up by roots. The nutrients cycle through the biomass, not the soil.

questions.q: Ocean acidification caused by rising atmospheric CO₂ poses the greatest direct threat to:
options: Photosynthetic phytoplankton that prefer acidic water
Coral reefs and shell-forming organisms whose calcium carbonate structures dissolve in acid
questions.explanation: CO₂ dissolves in seawater to form carbonic acid, lowering pH. Acidic water dissolves CaCO₃, the material in coral skeletons and mollusk shells, threatening entire reef and shellfish ecosystems.

questions.q: Which biome is characterized by permafrost, very low precipitation, and the absence of trees?
questions.explanation: The tundra has permanently frozen subsoil (permafrost) that prevents tree roots from establishing. Low precipitation, extreme cold, and short growing seasons support only mosses, lichens, and dwarf shrubs.

lessons.title: Animal Body Systems Overview
lessons.content:
Multicellular animals have specialized organ systems that maintain homeostasis — the relatively stable internal environment required for cellular function.

`Nervous system`: neurons transmit electrical signals (`action potentials`) via changes in Na⁺/K⁺ permeability. Signals cross synapses via neurotransmitters. Central nervous system (brain + spinal cord) integrates information; peripheral nervous system transmits it.

`Endocrine system`: glands secrete hormones into the blood. Hormones act on target cells bearing specific receptors. Examples: insulin (lowers blood glucose), glucagon (raises blood glucose) — antagonistic hormones from the pancreas. The hypothalamus-pituitary axis controls many glands.

`Circulatory system`: the heart pumps blood through arteries (from heart), capillaries (gas exchange), and veins (to heart). Mammals have a double circuit: pulmonary (heart → lungs → heart) and systemic (heart → body → heart). Blood carries O₂ (hemoglobin in red blood cells), nutrients, hormones, and immune cells.

`Immune system`: innate immunity (fast, non-specific — phagocytes, inflammation, fever). Adaptive immunity (slow, specific): B cells produce antibodies; T cells destroy infected cells. `Antigens` trigger the response; `memory cells` provide long-lasting protection (basis of vaccines).

`Digestive system`: mechanical and chemical digestion breaks food into absorbable monomers. Enzymes: amylase (starch → sugars), pepsin (proteins), lipase (fats). Small intestine absorbs nutrients via villi; large intestine absorbs water.

`Homeostasis` is maintained by `negative feedback`: a deviation from the set point triggers a response that reverses the change (e.g., blood glucose regulation, thermoregulation).

questions.q: After eating a carbohydrate-rich meal, blood glucose rises. The pancreas releases insulin. Insulin signals cells to take up glucose, lowering blood glucose to normal. This is an example of:
options: The endocrine system disrupting the nervous system
questions.explanation: Negative feedback reverses a deviation from the set point. Rising glucose → insulin release → glucose uptake → glucose returns to normal. The response counteracts the original change.

questions.q: Why are vaccines effective at providing long-term protection?
options: Vaccines introduce antibiotics that kill pathogens directly
They stimulate the innate immune system to produce fever permanently
They expose the immune system to an antigen, generating memory B and T cells that respond rapidly upon re-exposure
Vaccines suppress the immune system to prevent overreaction
questions.explanation: After a primary immune response (natural or vaccine-induced), memory cells persist. Upon re-exposure, they mount a faster, stronger secondary response — preventing disease before symptoms develop.

questions.q: Which digestive enzyme is produced in the stomach and begins protein digestion?
questions.explanation: Pepsin is the main gastric protease. It is secreted as inactive pepsinogen and activated by the stomach's acidic pH (~2). Amylase digests starch, lipase digests fats, lactase digests lactose.

questions.prompt: Complete the summary of energy transfer in photosynthesis:
questions.code:
Light energy is captured by ___ in the ___.
The Calvin cycle uses ___ and ___ to fix CO₂ into sugar.
questions.explanation: Chlorophyll absorbs light in the thylakoid membranes. The ATP and NADPH produced power the Calvin cycle in the stroma, where CO₂ is incorporated into G3P.

questions.prompt: Fill in the gene expression flow:
questions.code:
DNA → ___ → ___
This is called the ___ dogma of molecular biology.
questions.explanation: The central dogma: DNA is transcribed into RNA, which is translated into protein. Information flows in this direction (with some exceptions, e.g., retroviruses).

questions.code:
Only approximately ___% of energy is transferred between
trophic levels; the rest is lost as ___.
questions.explanation: The 10% rule: only ~10% of energy at one trophic level is available to the next. The remainder is lost through cellular respiration as heat, which limits food chain length.

questions.code:
Meiosis produces ___ genetically ___ haploid cells.
Genetic variation arises from crossing over and ___ assortment.
questions.explanation: Meiosis produces four haploid (n) cells. Crossing over (prophase I) and independent assortment (metaphase I) ensure that each is genetically unique.

lessons.title: Spot the Bug: Core Biology
lessons.prompt: The following statements summarize key biology concepts. One contains a factual error — find it.
lessons.code:
1. ATP is produced during cellular respiration and stores chemical energy.
2. Meiosis produces 4 haploid cells; mitosis produces 2 diploid cells.
3. The primary structure of a protein is its three-dimensional folded shape.
4. Producers occupy the first trophic level and are autotrophs.
5. DNA replication is semiconservative — each daughter strand retains one parental strand.
lessons.explanation: Line 3 is wrong. Primary structure is the linear sequence of amino acids (determined by the gene). Three-dimensional folded shape is tertiary structure. Secondary structure refers to alpha helices and beta sheets.
